question: How do enzymes catalyse reactions, and what does Michaelis-Menten describe?

options: Enzymes are biological catalysts that lower the activation energy by stabilising the transition state, vastly speeding reactions without being consumed; Michaelis-Menten kinetics gives rate $v = V_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$, where $K_m$ is the substrate concentration at half-maximal velocity
Enzymes increase activation energy to slow reactions (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Enzymes are consumed stoichiometrically in the reaction (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence)
Reaction rate is independent of substrate concentration (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Enzymes (mostly proteins, some RNA — ribozymes) are catalysts that accelerate biochemical reactions by factors of up to 10^17 without being consumed, by binding substrate at an active site and lowering the activation-energy barrier — chiefly by stabilising the reaction's transition state (and via proximity/orientation, acid-base and covalent catalysis, and strain). They do not change the reaction's equilibrium or thermodynamics, only its rate. Specificity arises from complementary active-site geometry and chemistry ('lock-and-key' refined to 'induced fit'). The Michaelis-Menten model describes the kinetics of many enzymes: at low substrate the rate rises nearly linearly with [S], saturating at $V_{max}$ when active sites are full; $v = V_{max}[S]/(K_m+[S])$. $K_m$ (the [S] giving half $V_{max}$) reflects substrate affinity, and $k_{cat}/K_m$ measures catalytic efficiency. Enzymes are regulated (allosteric effectors, covalent modification like phosphorylation, feedback inhibition) and inhibited (competitive, raising apparent $K_m$; noncompetitive, lowering $V_{max}$) — principles central to metabolism and to drug design (most drugs are enzyme inhibitors). Cofactors and coenzymes (metals, vitamins) assist many enzymes.

active.prompt: Match each biomolecule class with its role:

pairs.definition: Polymers of amino acids; enzymes, structure, transport, signalling; folded 3D shape determines function
Fats, phospholipids, steroids; membranes, energy storage, signalling hormones
DNA/RNA; store and transmit genetic information; some catalyse (ribozymes)

active.explanation: The four major classes of biomolecules are built from simple monomers (amino acids, monosaccharides, fatty acids/glycerol, nucleotides) into functional polymers and assemblies. Protein function follows from structure (primary sequence → secondary helices/sheets → tertiary fold → quaternary assembly). Water, with its hydrogen bonding and polarity, is the essential solvent shaping all of biochemistry (hydrophobic effect, pH).

question: Why is ATP the cell's energy currency?

options: Hydrolysis of ATP's high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds (ATP → ADP + Pi) releases ~30 kJ/mol under cellular conditions, which is coupled to drive otherwise unfavourable reactions; ATP is continually regenerated, linking energy-releasing (catabolic) to energy-requiring (anabolic) processes
ATP stores energy permanently and is never broken down (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
ATP provides energy by nuclear fission (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect)
ATP is a structural molecule with no energy role (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the universal energy carrier connecting catabolism (energy-releasing breakdown of fuels) to anabolism and cellular work. Its triphosphate tail holds two phosphoanhydride bonds whose hydrolysis is strongly exergonic (ΔG ≈ −30 kJ/mol under physiological conditions, more than the standard −30.5 kJ/mol because cellular ATP/ADP ratios are kept far from equilibrium) — driven by charge repulsion relief, resonance stabilisation of products, and solvation. Cells couple this favourable hydrolysis (or, more often, transfer of a phosphoryl group) to power unfavourable processes: biosynthesis, active transport (e.g. the Na+/K+ pump), muscle contraction (myosin), and signalling. ATP is not long-term storage but a rapidly cycled intermediate — a human turns over roughly their body weight in ATP per day, constantly regenerating it from ADP. That regeneration occurs by substrate-level phosphorylation (glycolysis, the citric-acid cycle) and, dominantly, oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria, where the electron-transport chain pumps protons to build an electrochemical gradient that ATP synthase uses to make ATP. ATP's central role across all life reflects deep evolutionary conservation.

question: How does cellular respiration extract energy from glucose?

options: Glycolysis splits glucose to pyruvate (cytoplasm, small ATP + NADH); the citric-acid (Krebs) cycle oxidises acetyl-CoA producing NADH/FADH2 and CO2; oxidative phosphorylation uses these electron carriers to drive an electron-transport chain and ATP synthase, yielding most ATP (~30+ per glucose)
Glucose is converted to ATP in a single step (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Respiration produces no carbon dioxide (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence)
All ATP comes from glycolysis alone (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: Aerobic cellular respiration harvests the energy in glucose through three coupled stages. (1) Glycolysis (cytoplasm) splits one glucose into two pyruvate, netting 2 ATP (substrate-level) and 2 NADH — anaerobic and ancient. (2) Pyruvate is oxidised to acetyl-CoA, which enters the citric-acid (Krebs/TCA) cycle in the mitochondrial matrix, fully oxidising the carbons to CO2 while harvesting high-energy electrons as NADH and FADH2 and a little GTP/ATP. (3) Oxidative phosphorylation (inner mitochondrial membrane): NADH and FADH2 donate electrons to the electron-transport chain, whose complexes pump protons into the intermembrane space, building an electrochemical proton gradient (Mitchell's chemiosmotic theory, Nobel 1978); protons flow back through ATP synthase, a molecular rotary motor, driving ATP synthesis, with O2 as the final electron acceptor (forming water). The full oxidation yields roughly 30-32 ATP per glucose — vastly more than fermentation's 2 — which is why aerobic metabolism powers complex life. Fats and proteins feed into the same pathways. This is the central energy-conversion machinery of the cell, mirrored (in reverse logic) by photosynthesis in plants.

question: What determines protein structure and why does folding matter?

options: The amino-acid sequence (primary structure) dictates folding into secondary (alpha-helices, beta-sheets via backbone H-bonds), tertiary (3D fold from side-chain interactions and the hydrophobic effect), and quaternary structure; the folded shape determines function, and misfolding causes disease
Protein function is independent of 3D shape (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Proteins are always linear chains that never fold (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Sequence has no influence on structure (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence)
passive.explanation: A protein's function is inseparable from its three-dimensional shape, which is encoded by its amino-acid sequence (Anfinsen's principle: sequence determines fold). Structure is described hierarchically: primary (the linear sequence of the 20 amino acids), secondary (local backbone motifs — alpha-helices and beta-sheets stabilised by hydrogen bonds), tertiary (the overall 3D fold of a polypeptide, driven largely by burial of hydrophobic side chains away from water — the hydrophobic effect — plus hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, disulfide bonds, and van der Waals contacts), and quaternary (assembly of multiple subunits, e.g. haemoglobin's four chains). The folded native state is typically the thermodynamic free-energy minimum, reached rapidly despite the astronomical number of possible conformations (Levinthal's paradox, resolved by funnelled energy landscapes); chaperones assist folding in the crowded cell. Misfolding and aggregation cause major diseases — amyloid plaques in Alzheimer's, prion diseases, and many others. Predicting structure from sequence was a grand challenge essentially solved by deep learning (AlphaFold, 2020-2021), transforming biology by providing accurate models for nearly all known proteins and accelerating drug discovery and enzyme design.

question: How does photosynthesis convert light into chemical energy?

options: Light-dependent reactions in the thylakoid use chlorophyll to split water, release O2, and make ATP and NADPH via an electron-transport chain; the light-independent Calvin cycle then fixes CO2 into sugars using that ATP/NADPH (RuBisCO catalysing carbon fixation)
Plants make sugar directly from sunlight with no intermediate chemistry (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception)
Photosynthesis consumes oxygen and releases carbon dioxide (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
It occurs without any pigments or enzymes (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Photosynthesis is the energy-input engine of the biosphere, converting solar energy into chemical bonds (net: 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light → glucose + 6 O2). It proceeds in two coupled stages in chloroplasts. The light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes): chlorophyll and accessory pigments in photosystems II and I absorb photons, exciting electrons; photosystem II extracts replacement electrons by splitting water (the source of atmospheric O2 and of the protons for the gradient), and the electrons pass down an electron-transport chain that pumps protons, driving ATP synthase (photophosphorylation) and ultimately reducing NADP+ to NADPH. The light-independent reactions (the Calvin-Benson cycle, in the stroma) then use that ATP and NADPH to fix inorganic CO2 onto the 5-carbon sugar RuBP via the enzyme RuBisCO (the most abundant protein on Earth), producing 3-carbon sugars that build glucose and regenerate RuBP. RuBisCO's inefficiency and oxygenase side-reaction (photorespiration) spurred the evolution of C4 and CAM carbon-concentrating adaptations in plants of hot/dry climates. Photosynthesis created Earth's oxygen atmosphere (the Great Oxidation Event), underpins nearly all food chains, and is the basis of fossil fuels — making its mechanism central to biology, agriculture, and climate.

question: How are metabolic pathways regulated and integrated?

options: Through allosteric regulation, feedback inhibition (end-product inhibits an early enzyme), reversible covalent modification (phosphorylation), hormonal control (insulin/glucagon), and compartmentalisation; this keeps catabolism and anabolism balanced and responsive to the cell's energy state (e.g. ATP/AMP, NAD+/NADH ratios)
Metabolism runs at constant maximum rate at all times (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Pathways operate completely independently with no coordination (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship)
There is no regulation of metabolic flux (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Metabolism is a tightly integrated network, not a set of isolated pathways, and is regulated to match supply and demand while avoiding futile cycles. Key control strategies: allosteric regulation (effectors bind enzymes away from the active site to change activity — e.g. ATP inhibits and AMP activates phosphofructokinase, the key glycolytic control point, tying flux to energy charge); feedback (end-product) inhibition (a pathway's product inhibits its committed/first enzyme, preventing overproduction); reversible covalent modification (phosphorylation/dephosphorylation by kinases/phosphatases rapidly switches enzymes, central to hormone signalling cascades); transcriptional control of enzyme amounts (slower, adaptive); and substrate availability and compartmentalisation (separating opposing pathways in different organelles, e.g. fatty-acid synthesis in cytosol vs oxidation in mitochondria). Whole-body integration is hormonal: insulin (fed state) promotes storage/anabolism, glucagon and adrenaline (fasting/stress) mobilise fuels; master sensors like AMPK (low energy) and mTOR (growth signals) coordinate metabolism with nutrient status. The ratios of key indicators — ATP/ADP/AMP, NADH/NAD+, acetyl-CoA — act as readouts of metabolic state. This regulation maintains homeostasis (e.g. blood glucose), and its breakdown underlies diabetes, obesity, and metabolic disease; cancer cells famously rewire metabolism (the Warburg effect).
